 {"id":"brenner_bot-a9d","title":"Component: Jargon tooltip with desktop hover + mobile bottom sheet","description":"# Jargon Component Implementation

## Goal
Create a reusable `<Jargon>` component that displays inline technical terms with:
- Desktop: Hover tooltip via React portal
- Mobile: Tap triggers slide-up bottom sheet
- Accessible: ARIA, keyboard nav, focus management

## Component API

```tsx
interface JargonProps {
  /** Term key to look up in dictionary */
  term: string;
  /** Override display text (defaults to term.term) */
  children?: ReactNode;
  /** Additional styling */
  className?: string;
}

// Usage:
<Jargon term="c-elegans">C. elegans</Jargon>
<Jargon term="level-split" />
```

## Desktop Tooltip Behavior
1. On hover/focus, show floating tooltip above term
2. Tooltip contains: term name, short definition, analogy if present
3. Position: prefer above, flip below if near top of viewport
4. Render via React portal to escape stacking context issues
5. Close on mouse leave with 150ms delay (allows moving to tooltip)

## Mobile Bottom Sheet Behavior
1. On tap, show slide-up bottom sheet with backdrop
2. Sheet contains: full term name, short + long definition, analogy, why, related
3. Lock body scroll while open
4. Close on backdrop tap or X button
5. Smooth spring animation respecting `prefers-reduced-motion`

## Styling (match existing design system)
- Trigger: `decoration-primary/30 decoration-dotted underline underline-offset-[3px]`
- Hover: `hover:decoration-primary/60 hover:text-primary/90`
- Tooltip: `rounded-xl border border-border/50 bg-card/95 backdrop-blur-xl shadow-2xl`
- Sheet: `rounded-t-3xl` with gradient accent line at top
- Use existing CSS animations from globals.css

## Key Implementation Details
- Use `createPortal` for tooltip/sheet to escape overflow:hidden ancestors
- Detect mobile via `window.matchMedia("(max-width: 768px)")`
- Clean up timeouts on unmount
- Handle unknown terms gracefully (render children without styling)

## Files
- `apps/web/src/components/jargon.tsx`
- Depends on: `lib/jargon.ts` (schema bead)

## Testing
- Unit test: render known term, verify tooltip appears
- Unit test: unknown term renders children without styling
- Manual test: hover/tap across breakpoints","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-30T00:57:37.260794075-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-30T01:05:37.366602282-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-30T01:05:37.366602282-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented Jargon component with desktop hover tooltip (portal-based) and mobile bottom sheet with swipe-to-dismiss. Features: ARIA, keyboard nav, reduced-motion support, glossary deep links. All 545 tests pass.","labels":["component","glossary","mobile"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-a9d","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-64f","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-30T01:02:54.506232128-05:00","created_by":"daemon","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"brenner_bot-aeu","title":"TanStack Store: Migrate useLocalStorage Hook to Store","description":"# Goal
Migrate the existing useLocalStorage hook to use TanStack Store for consistency.

## Background
We have a custom useLocalStorage hook:
```typescript
// apps/web/src/hooks/useLocalStorage.ts
export function useLocalStorage<T>(key: string, initialValue: T) {
  const [storedValue, setStoredValue] = useState<T>(() => {
    try {
      const item = window.localStorage.getItem(key);
      return item ? JSON.parse(item) : initialValue;
    } catch {
      return initialValue;
    }
  });
  // ...
}
```

After implementing TanStack Store for reading positions and compare state, we should:
1. Evaluate if useLocalStorage is still needed
2. Either delete it or have it use Store internally
3. Ensure consistency across all persisted state

## Decision Matrix

| Use Case | Current | After TanStack Store |
|----------|---------|---------------------|
| Reading positions | N/A (none) | readingStore |
| Compare state | N/A (none) | compareStore |
| User preferences | useLocalStorage | preferencesStore |
| Form drafts | N/A | Could use Store |

## Options

### Option A: Delete useLocalStorage
If all use cases are covered by specific stores, delete the generic hook.
Simpler, but less flexible.

### Option B: Keep as Utility
Keep useLocalStorage for one-off values that don't need a store.
E.g., "dismissed welcome banner" flag.

### Option C: Make Store-backed
Reimplement useLocalStorage to use a generic Store internally.
Best of both worlds.

## Recommendation
Option B: Keep useLocalStorage for simple boolean flags, use Store for complex state.

## Migration Steps
1. Audit all uses of useLocalStorage
2. Move complex state to dedicated stores
3. Keep hook for simple flags
4. Document when to use which

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All complex persisted state uses TanStack Store
- [ ] useLocalStorage only used for simple values
- [ ] No duplicate persistence logic
- [ ] Clear documentation on when to use what

## Files to Evaluate
- apps/web/src/hooks/useLocalStorage.ts
- Any component using useLocalStorage","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-29T23:20:36.787601565-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-30T00:32:51.654878107-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-30T00:32:51.654878107-05:00","close_reason":"Migration complete: Removed duplicate useReadingPosition from useLocalStorage.ts (TanStack Store version in useReadingPosition.ts is the canonical one). Added documentation explaining when to use useLocalStorage (simple flags) vs TanStack Store (complex state). The readingStore pattern is already established and working.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-aeu","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-1uu","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-29T23:20:44.030717303-05:00","created_by":"daemon","metadata":"{}"}]}

+{"id":"brenner_bot-aitt","title":"Manual a11y audit for SessionDashboard shortcuts","description":"# Manual a11y audit for `SessionDashboard` shortcuts

## Why
We added keyboard shortcuts + screen-reader affordances to `SessionDashboard` (shortcuts dialog, phase nav hotkeys, skip link, aria-live).

## What to verify
- Keyboard-only flow:
  - `?` opens/closes shortcuts dialog
  - `←`/`→` phase navigation (when not typing)
  - `1-9` phase jump (when reachable)
  - `Cmd/Ctrl+S` saves (even while typing)
  - `Cmd/Ctrl+E` exports (Shift exports JSON)
- Focus management:
  - Dialog focus trap + restores focus on close
  - Skip link appears on Tab and jumps to main
- Screen reader:
  - `aria-live` announces phase changes
  - `aria-keyshortcuts` is present on key controls
- Visual/accessibility:
  - Focus rings visible on interactive controls
  - Contrast meets WCAG AA (incl. muted text)
  - States are distinguishable without color

## How
- Add/extend a Playwright test using `@axe-core/playwright` for the session dashboard route once it exists.
- Manual smoke check with VoiceOver (macOS) and NVDA (Windows).

 {"id":"brenner_bot-aly1","title":"Spec: External Evidence Import Protocol","description":"# Goal
Define the protocol for importing external evidence (papers, datasets, prior experiment results, benchmark data) into Brenner Loop sessions as citable, structured inputs.

## The Gap This Fills

Other agents covered:
- **Topic corpus ingestion** - PRIMARY sources for a research domain (like Brenner transcripts for the method itself)
- **Results → DELTA encoding** - How OUR experiment outputs become deltas
- **Target repository binding** - How sessions connect to codebases

**What nobody covered:** How do EXTERNAL evidence sources enter the system? Specifically:

1. A paper was published last week that refutes hypothesis H2 - how does that enter the session?
2. A benchmark dataset exists that could serve as a potency check - how do we reference it?
3. A colleague ran an experiment outside this system and has results - how do we import them?
4. A prior Brenner Loop session (different project) produced relevant findings - how do we cite it?

This is different from "topic corpus" because:
- Topic corpus = domain knowledge you READ to inform hypotheses
- External evidence = RESULTS that validate/refute hypotheses

## Why This Matters for Real Science

Real research constantly references external evidence:
- "Smith et al. (2024) showed X, which supports H1"
- "The ImageNet benchmark demonstrates Y"
- "Our collaborators experiment found Z"

Without a formal import mechanism, agents can only:
1. Reference their training data (not auditable, may be stale)
2. Hallucinate citations (dangerous)
3. Ignore external evidence entirely (limiting)

## Proposed Solution

### 1. Evidence Types

```typescript
type EvidenceType = 
  | "paper"           // Published research paper
  | "preprint"        // Unpublished manuscript
  | "dataset"         // Structured data (benchmark, corpus, etc.)
  | "experiment"      // Results from an experiment (internal or external)
  | "observation"     // Empirical observation without formal experiment
  | "prior_session"   // Results from another Brenner Loop session
  | "expert_opinion"  // Human expert statement
  | "code_artifact";  // Existing code/implementation as evidence
```

### 2. Evidence Record Structure

```typescript
interface ExternalEvidence {
  id: string;           // E.g., "EV-001" or DOI
  type: EvidenceType;
  
  // Citation info
  title: string;
  authors?: string[];
  date: string;
  source: string;       // URL, DOI, file path, or session ID
  
  // Access
  access_method: "url" | "file" | "agent_mail" | "api";
  access_path: string;
  
  // Content summary
  abstract?: string;    // Human-readable summary
  key_findings: string[]; // Bulleted findings relevant to session
  
  // Relationship to session
  imported_at: string;
  imported_by: string;  // Agent or operator who added it
  relevance: string;    // Why this evidence matters to this session
  
  // Targeting
  supports?: string[];  // Item IDs this evidence supports (e.g., ["H1", "T3"])
  refutes?: string[];   // Item IDs this evidence refutes
  informs?: string[];   // Item IDs this evidence is relevant to
  
  // Verification
  verified: boolean;    // Has someone confirmed this evidence is real/accurate?
  verification_notes?: string;
}
```

### 3. Import Protocol

#### Via CLI:
```bash
# Import a paper by DOI
brenner evidence import --type paper --doi "10.1234/example" --relevance "Supports H1 mechanism"

# Import experiment results from file
brenner evidence import --type experiment --file results.json --supports H1,H4 --refutes H2

# Import from prior session
brenner evidence import --type prior_session --session "RS-20251228-initial" --item H3
```

#### Via Agent Mail:
```markdown
Subject: EVIDENCE: External paper supports H1

```evidence
{
  "type": "paper",
  "title": "Synaptic vesicle depletion dynamics in attention mechanisms",
  "doi": "10.1234/example",
  "key_findings": [
    "RRP depletion follows exponential decay",
    "Recovery time constant ~500ms matches our T1 predictions"
  ],
  "supports": ["H1"],
  "relevance": "Provides empirical basis for vesicle depletion timescales"
}
```​
```

### 4. Evidence in Artifacts

Artifacts gain a new section:

```markdown
## External Evidence

| ID | Type | Source | Supports | Refutes | Key Finding |
|----|------|--------|----------|---------|-------------|
| EV-001 | paper | doi:10.1234/ex | H1 | - | RRP decay matches predictions |
| EV-002 | dataset | benchmarks/synthetic_rep.json | T5 | - | Provides test stimuli |
| EV-003 | prior_session | RS-20251228-initial | - | H2 | H2 was killed after T3 |
```

### 5. Evidence Validation

Agents and operators can challenge evidence:
- "Is this paper peer-reviewed?"
- "Is this dataset properly documented?"
- "Can we verify these experiment results?"

Unverified evidence is marked and treated with lower confidence.

### 6. Integration with Deltas

Deltas can reference evidence:

```json
{
  "operation": "KILL",
  "section": "hypothesis_slate",
  "target_id": "H2",
  "payload": {
    "kill_reason": "Contradicted by external evidence EV-001 and EV-003"
  },
  "evidence_refs": ["EV-001", "EV-003"]
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `ExternalEvidence` type defined in session/artifact types
- [ ] Evidence section added to artifact schema
- [ ] CLI `brenner evidence import` command with --type, --source, --supports, --refutes
- [ ] Agent Mail EVIDENCE: message type with structured payload parsing
- [ ] Evidence references allowed in delta blocks
- [ ] Artifact lint validates evidence references (warns on missing EV-xxx)
- [ ] Web UI shows evidence in session detail
- [ ] Evidence verification workflow (mark verified/unverified)

## Dependencies

- Artifact schema (exists)
- Delta parser (needs extension for evidence_refs)
- Independent of experiment execution (evidence can be imported without running experiments)

## Non-Goals

- Not building a full citation manager (use Zotero/etc. for that)
- Not automatically fetching paper content (just referencing)
- Not implementing evidence strength scoring (thats evaluation rubric territory)

## Example Use Case

Research question: Does vesicle depletion in bio_inspired_nanochat behave like biological synapses?

1. Operator imports paper on synaptic vesicle dynamics (EV-001)
2. Operator imports benchmark dataset for repetition detection (EV-002)
3. Agent references EV-001 in proposing H1 ("our implementation matches biological timescales")
4. Another agent challenges: "EV-001 was in vitro, not in silico - relevance unclear"
5. Test T4 designed to compare our implementation against EV-002 dataset
6. Results of T4 imported as EV-003, which refutes H2
7. Final artifact shows complete evidence chain for conclusions","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-30T19:02:25.471441949-05:00","updated_at":"2026-01-01T14:30:52.253072099-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-01T14:30:52.253072099-05:00","close_reason":"Spec fully written in specs/evidence_pack_v0.1.md. Implementation tracked by brenner_bot-5so.13 and children. Core acceptance criteria met: ExternalEvidence types defined, CLI commands implemented, linter supports citations, evidence_refs in deltas specified. Web UI viewer is separate feature (5so.13.4).","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-aly1","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-5so.13","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-12-30T19:34:56.888101882-05:00","created_by":"daemon","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"brenner_bot-an1n","title":"EPIC: Hypothesis Engine - Core Data Models & Evolution Tracking","description":"# Hypothesis Engine - Core Data Models & Evolution Tracking

## Purpose
The Hypothesis Engine is the foundational data layer for the entire Brenner Loop system. It defines how hypotheses are represented, stored, evolved, and tracked over time.

## Why This Matters
Most research tools treat hypotheses as static text strings. The Brenner approach requires understanding hypotheses as **living, evolving structures** with:
- Explicit predictions (what would be true if hypothesis is correct)
- Explicit falsification conditions (what would prove it wrong)
- Version history (how thinking evolved and why)
- Confidence tracking (updated by evidence)
- Structural quality metrics (is it actually testable?)

## Core Data Structures

### HypothesisCard
The primary unit of the system. Each hypothesis becomes a card with:
```typescript
interface HypothesisCard {
  id: string;
  version: number;
  
  // Core statement
  statement: string;  // Formalized hypothesis
  mechanism: string;  // Proposed causal pathway
  domain: string[];   // Fields this touches (psychology, epidemiology, etc.)
  
  // Discriminative structure (the key innovation)
  predictionsIfTrue: string[];   // What we'd see if correct
  predictionsIfFalse: string[];  // What we'd see if wrong
  impossibleIfTrue: string[];    // Observations that would FALSIFY
  
  // Identified weaknesses
  confounds: IdentifiedConfound[];
  assumptions: string[];
  
  // Tracking
  confidence: number;  // 0-100, updated by evidence
  parentVersion?: string;
  evolutionReason?: string;
  
  // Timestamps
  createdAt: Date;
  updatedAt: Date;
}
```

### HypothesisEvolution
Tracks the lineage of how hypotheses transform:
```typescript
interface HypothesisEvolution {
  id: string;
  fromVersion: string;
  toVersion: string;
  reason: string;  // Why did thinking change?
  trigger: 'level_split' | 'exclusion_test' | 'evidence' | 'agent_feedback' | 'manual';
  timestamp: Date;
}
```

### RobustnessScore
Quantifies how well a hypothesis has survived testing:
```typescript
interface RobustnessScore {
  overall: number;  // 0-100
  testsAttempted: number;
  testsSurvived: number;
  testsFailed: number;
  weightedSurvival: number;  // Weighted by discriminative power
  falsifiabilityScore: number;  // How testable is it?
  specificityScore: number;     // How precise is the prediction?
  interpretation: 'robust' | 'shaky' | 'falsified' | 'untested';
}
```

## Key Design Decisions

1. **Immutable Versions**: Hypotheses are never edited in place. Each change creates a new version with a link to the parent. This preserves intellectual history.

2. **Explicit Falsifiability**: The `impossibleIfTrue` field forces users to articulate what would disprove their hypothesis. This is the core Brenner insight operationalized.

3. **Domain-Aware Confounds**: Different fields have different common confounds. The system should surface domain-relevant pitfalls automatically.

4. **Confidence as Bayesian Update**: Each piece of evidence adjusts confidence based on the test's discriminative power, not just whether it "supports" the hypothesis.

## Dependencies
- None (this is foundational)

## Success Criteria
- Users can create hypothesis cards through guided intake
- Hypothesis versions are automatically tracked
- Robustness scores update as evidence accumulates
- The evolution graph is navigable and useful

## Future Extensions
- Hypothesis networks (how hypotheses relate to each other)
- Collaborative hypothesis refinement
- Learning from hypothesis failure patterns

 {"id":"brenner_bot-reew.3","title":"Implement Session State Machine","description":"# Implement Session State Machine

## Purpose
Create a formal state machine that governs session phase transitions.

## Why a State Machine
Session progression has complex rules:
- Some phases depend on others
- Some phases can be skipped
- Some phases can be revisited
- Invalid transitions should be impossible

A state machine makes this explicit and prevents bugs.

## State Definition
```typescript
const sessionMachine = createMachine({
  id: 'session',
  initial: 'intake',
  
  states: {
    intake: {
      on: { SUBMIT_HYPOTHESIS: 'sharpening' }
    },
    
    sharpening: {
      on: {
        REFINE: 'sharpening',  // Stay and refine more
        CONTINUE: 'level_split',
        SKIP_OPERATORS: 'agent_dispatch'
      }
    },
    
    level_split: {
      on: {
        COMPLETE: 'exclusion_test',
        SKIP: 'exclusion_test',
        BACK: 'sharpening'
      }
    },
    
    exclusion_test: {
      on: {
        COMPLETE: 'object_transpose',
        SKIP: 'object_transpose',
        BACK: 'level_split'
      }
    },
    
    object_transpose: {
      on: {
        COMPLETE: 'scale_check',
        SKIP: 'scale_check',
        BACK: 'exclusion_test'
      }
    },
    
    scale_check: {
      on: {
        COMPLETE: 'agent_dispatch',
        SKIP: 'agent_dispatch',
        BACK: 'object_transpose'
      }
    },
    
    agent_dispatch: {
      on: {
        RESPONSES_RECEIVED: 'synthesis',
        SKIP_AGENTS: 'evidence_gathering',
        BACK: 'scale_check'
      }
    },
    
    synthesis: {
      on: {
        CONTINUE: 'evidence_gathering',
        BACK: 'agent_dispatch'
      }
    },
    
    evidence_gathering: {
      on: {
        ADD_EVIDENCE: 'evidence_gathering',
        REVISE_HYPOTHESIS: 'revision',
        COMPLETE: 'complete',
        BACK: 'synthesis'
      }
    },
    
    revision: {
      on: {
        SAVE: 'evidence_gathering',
        RESTART_OPERATORS: 'level_split'
      }
    },
    
    complete: {
      type: 'final'
    }
  }
});
```

## Integration
Use XState or similar, or custom implementation:
```typescript
function useSessionMachine(initialState: SessionPhase) {
  // Returns current state, available transitions, send function
}
```

## File Location
`apps/web/src/lib/brenner-loop/session-machine.ts`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All states and transitions defined
- [ ] Invalid transitions impossible
- [ ] State persists with session
- [ ] Hook provides clean API

Includes: all 11 phases as states, SessionEvent types, transition guards (hasPredictions, hasPrimaryHypothesis, etc.), transition() function, useSessionMachine hook with React integration, convenience actions (advance, goBack, goToPhase), phase display helpers. All acceptance criteria met.","labels":["brenner-loop","session-ui","state-machine"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-reew.3","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-reew","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-04T02:34:27.26490142-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"brenner_bot-reew.4","title":"Create Session List & Management Page","description":"# Create Session List & Management Page

## Purpose
Build the page where users see all their sessions and can create new ones.

## Page Layout
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  BrennerBot Lab                           [+ New Session]       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                 │
│  Your Research Sessions                                         │
│  ═══════════════════════                                        │
│                                                                 │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ SM-2024-003                              2 hours ago    │   │
│  │ "Algorithm-driven content causes teen depression"       │   │
│  │ Phase: Evidence Gathering │ Confidence: 37% │ v3        │   │
│  │ [Continue] [Export] [Delete]                            │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                 │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ SM-2024-002                              1 day ago      │   │
│  │ "Meditation reduces anxiety through attention training"  │   │
│  │ Phase: Complete │ Confidence: 72% │ v2                  │   │
│  │ [View Brief] [Export] [Delete]                          │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                 │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ SM-2024-001                              3 days ago     │   │
│  │ "Remote work improves productivity"                     │   │
│  │ Phase: Exclusion Test │ Confidence: 65% │ v1            │   │
│  │ [Continue] [Export] [Delete]                            │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                 │
│  [Load More]                                                    │
│                                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Features

### Session Card
Shows:
- Session ID
- Hypothesis preview
- Current phase
- Confidence score
- Last updated
- Quick actions

### Filtering & Sorting
- Sort by: Date, Confidence, Phase
- Filter by: Status (active, complete), Confidence range

### Actions
- **Continue**: Resume in-progress session
- **View Brief**: For complete sessions
- **Export**: Download as Markdown/PDF/JSON
- **Delete**: With confirmation

### New Session Flow
Click "+ New Session" → Hypothesis Intake form

## Route
`/lab` or `/sessions`

## File Location
`apps/web/src/app/lab/page.tsx`
`apps/web/src/components/brenner-loop/SessionCard.tsx`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Sessions listed with all info
- [ ] Sorting and filtering work
- [ ] Actions work correctly
- [ ] Delete has confirmation
- [ ] Pagination/infinite scroll works

 {"id":"brenner_bot-reew.5","title":"Implement Mobile-Responsive Session Experience","description":"# Implement Mobile-Responsive Session Experience

## Purpose
Ensure the full session experience works well on mobile devices.

## Mobile Challenges
1. Limited horizontal space
2. Touch-based interaction
3. Keyboard covers content
4. Multiple panels don't fit side-by-side

## Mobile Adaptations

### Phase Timeline
- Switch to vertical stepper
- Or compact horizontal with icons only
- Swipe to navigate phases

### Session Dashboard
- Stack panels vertically
- Hypothesis card as collapsible header
- Brenner quotes in expandable section

### Operator Sessions
- Full-width content area
- Step navigation via swipe or buttons
- Quote sidebar becomes bottom sheet

### Agent Tribunal
- Agents in accordion/tabs
- One agent visible at a time
- Expand to see full response

### Evidence Recording
- Full-screen modal
- Multi-step as swipeable pages

## Implementation Approach
- Use Tailwind responsive utilities
- Consider mobile-first CSS
- Test on real devices

## Touch Interactions
- Swipe to navigate
- Long-press for context menus
- Pull-to-refresh for agent updates

## File Location
Various components with responsive variants.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All phases work on mobile
- [ ] No horizontal scrolling required
- [ ] Touch interactions feel native
- [ ] Text is readable
- [ ] Actions are tappable

-{"id":"brenner_bot-reew.6","title":"Add Keyboard Navigation & Accessibility","description":"# Add Keyboard Navigation & Accessibility

## Purpose
Make the session experience fully accessible via keyboard and screen readers.

## Keyboard Navigation

### Global Shortcuts
- `?`: Show keyboard shortcuts
- `Esc`: Close modal/go back
- `Cmd/Ctrl + S`: Save session
- `Cmd/Ctrl + E`: Export

### Phase Navigation
- `←` `→`: Previous/Next phase
- `1-9`: Jump to phase by number
- `Tab`: Navigate within phase

### Within Forms
- `Tab`: Next field
- `Shift+Tab`: Previous field
- `Enter`: Submit/Continue
- `Space`: Toggle checkboxes

### Hypothesis Card
- `E`: Edit hypothesis
- `H`: View history
- `V`: Expand/collapse

## Focus Management
- Clear focus indicators (visible rings)
- Focus trapped in modals
- Focus restored after modal close
- Skip links for screen readers

## Screen Reader Support
- Semantic HTML (headings, landmarks)
- ARIA labels for interactive elements
- Live regions for dynamic updates
- Descriptive alt text

## Color Contrast
- All text meets WCAG AA
- States distinguishable without color
- High contrast mode support

## Testing
- Test with VoiceOver (Mac)
- Test with NVDA (Windows)
- Test keyboard-only navigation
- Run axe-core accessibility audit

## File Location
Various components with accessibility improvements.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All interactions keyboard-accessible
- [ ] Focus management correct
- [ ] Screen reader tested
- [ ] axe-core passes

 {"id":"brenner_bot-reew.7","title":"FEATURE: Session Templates - Quick Check, Full Analysis, Literature Review","description":"# Session Templates - Quick Check, Full Analysis, Literature Review

## The Gap This Fills
Currently, all sessions follow the same full flow. But users have different needs:
- Quick sanity check of an idea
- Full rigorous analysis
- Literature-focused evaluation
- Design-focused session

Different goals need different session structures.

## Feature Design

### Session Template Structure
```typescript
interface SessionTemplate {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  description: string;
  icon: string;
  
  // Phase configuration
  requiredPhases: SessionPhase[];
  optionalPhases: SessionPhase[];
  skippedPhases: SessionPhase[];
  
  // Default settings
  agentsToDispatch: AgentRole[];
  defaultDepth: 'quick' | 'standard' | 'deep';
  
  // Guidance
  expectedDuration: string;
  bestFor: string[];
  
  // Customization
  allowCustomization: boolean;
}
```

### Pre-Built Templates

#### 1. Quick Check (15-30 min)
```
Purpose: Fast sanity check of an idea
Phases: intake → exclusion_test → synthesis
Agents: Devil's Advocate only
Best for: Early-stage ideas, gut checks

"Is this idea worth pursuing? Get quick pushback."
```

#### 2. Full Analysis (2-4 hours)
```
Purpose: Rigorous comprehensive analysis
Phases: All phases enabled
Agents: All agents
Best for: Important hypotheses, formal research

"Leave no stone unturned. Full Brenner treatment."
```

#### 3. Literature Review (1-2 hours)
```
Purpose: Evaluate hypothesis against existing research
Phases: intake → exclusion_test → evidence_gathering
Agents: None (or just Experiment Designer for search guidance)
Best for: When you want to check existing literature

"What does the literature already say about this?"
```

#### 4. Design Focus (1-2 hours)
```
Purpose: Design experiments to test hypothesis
Phases: intake → level_split → exclusion_test → agent_dispatch
Agents: Experiment Designer only
Best for: Planning research, grant proposals

"What experiments would test this properly?"
```

#### 5. Adversarial Deep Dive (2-3 hours)
```
Purpose: Maximum adversarial pressure
Phases: intake → all operators → agent_dispatch → debate
Agents: All agents + debate mode
Best for: High-stakes hypotheses, preparing for critics

"Try to destroy this hypothesis before critics do."
```

### Template Selection UI
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Choose Session Type                                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                             │
│  ⚡ QUICK CHECK                          ~20 min           │
│  Fast sanity check. Is this worth pursuing?                │
│  [Select]                                                   │
│                                                             │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
│                                                             │
│  🔬 FULL ANALYSIS                        ~3 hours          │
│  Complete Brenner Loop treatment. All operators & agents.  │
│  [Select]                                                   │
│                                                             │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
│                                                             │
│  📚 LITERATURE REVIEW                    ~90 min           │
│  Evaluate against existing research.                       │
│  [Select]                                                   │
│                                                             │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
│                                                             │
│  🧪 DESIGN FOCUS                         ~90 min           │
│  Plan experiments to test hypothesis.                       │
│  [Select]                                                   │
│                                                             │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
│                                                             │
│  ⚔️ ADVERSARIAL DEEP DIVE                ~2 hours          │
│  Maximum challenge. Try to destroy this hypothesis.        │
│  [Select]                                                   │
│                                                             │
│  [Custom Session...] (configure your own phases)            │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Integration Points
- Session Start → Template selection
- State Machine → Configured by template
- Phase Timeline → Shows template-specific phases
- Research Brief → Notes which template was used

## Why This is Critical
Without templates:
- Users face intimidating full flow for simple questions
- Quick checks take too long
- Different use cases force same process

With templates:
- Right-sized process for each need
- Lower barrier to entry
- More sessions run (users aren't scared off)
- Better fit between goal and method

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 5+ templates available
- [ ] Template configures state machine
- [ ] Users can create custom templates
- [ ] Template choice recorded in session
- [ ] Can change template mid-session (with warning)

 {"id":"brenner_bot-reew.8","title":"FEATURE: Guided Coach Mode - Enhanced Scaffolding for New Users","description":"# Guided Coach Mode - Enhanced Scaffolding for New Users

## The Gap This Fills
New users may be overwhelmed by:
- The methodology itself
- What each operator does
- How to formulate good hypotheses
- What "discriminative testing" means

Need a "training wheels" mode that teaches as it guides.

## Feature Design

### Coach Mode Overlay
```typescript
interface CoachSettings {
  enabled: boolean;
  level: 'beginner' | 'intermediate' | 'advanced';
  
  // What to show
  showExamples: boolean;
  showExplanations: boolean;
  showBrenerQuotes: boolean;
  showProgressTips: boolean;
  
  // Interactivity
  pauseForExplanation: boolean;
  requireConfirmation: boolean;
}
```

### Key Features

#### 1. Inline Explanations
Every concept gets an explanation:
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  EXCLUSION TEST                                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                             │
│  💡 COACH: What is an Exclusion Test?                       │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │ Most research asks: "Is there evidence for my idea?" │ │
│  │                                                        │ │
│  │ The Exclusion Test asks: "What would PROVE ME WRONG?" │ │
│  │                                                        │ │
│  │ This is Brenner's key insight: Good hypotheses make   │ │
│  │ bold predictions that could fail. If you can't        │ │
│  │ imagine being proven wrong, your hypothesis isn't     │ │
│  │ scientific — it's just an opinion.                    │ │
│  │                                                        │ │
│  │ "I'm going to help you generate tests that could     │ │
│  │  actually falsify your hypothesis."                   │ │
│  │                                                        │ │
│  │ [Got it!] [Show me an example] [Why does this matter?]│ │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                             │
│  [Continue to Exclusion Test...]                            │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

#### 2. Worked Examples
For each phase, show a complete example:
- "Here's how a psychology hypothesis went through Level Split"
- "This is what a good exclusion test looks like"
- "See how this hypothesis was refined after agent feedback"

Example bank organized by domain:
- Psychology examples
- Epidemiology examples
- Economics examples
- etc.

#### 3. Quality Checkpoints
Coach pauses to check understanding:
```
💡 COACH: Let's check your hypothesis formulation

Your hypothesis: "Social media causes depression"

Issues I see:
⚠️ No specific mechanism proposed
⚠️ "Social media" is too broad (which platforms? what behavior?)
⚠️ "Depression" unspecified (clinical? self-reported?)

Would you like help refining this?
[Yes, help me] [No, I'll continue as-is]
```

#### 4. Progress Encouragement
Celebrate learning moments:
- "Great! You identified a falsification condition."
- "You just applied Object Transpose — considering alternatives is crucial."
- "This is a strong exclusion test — high discriminative power."

#### 5. Mistake Prevention
Warn before common errors:
- "You're about to skip Level Split. 40% of failed hypotheses had confounded levels."
- "Your falsification condition sounds like 'supporting evidence' — flip it around."
- "This test has low discriminative power. Consider a stronger test."

#### 6. Gradual Scaffolding Removal
As user completes sessions:
- First 3 sessions: Full coach mode
- Sessions 4-10: Reduced explanations
- After 10: Coach available on demand

Track which explanations user has seen:
- Don't repeat explanations unnecessarily
- Remember user's level
- Adapt to demonstrated competence

### Coach Personas (Future)
Different coaching styles:
- "Sydney Brenner" - Blunt and challenging
- "Supportive Teacher" - Patient and encouraging  
- "Research Mentor" - Professional and thorough

## Why This is Critical
Without coaching:
- New users don't understand the methodology
- They make avoidable mistakes
- The system feels intimidating
- Dropout rate is high

With coaching:
- Learning is built into the experience
- Users understand WHY, not just WHAT
- Mistakes are caught early
- Users develop genuine capability

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Coach mode toggleable
- [ ] Inline explanations for all concepts
- [ ] Worked examples for each phase
- [ ] Quality checkpoints catch issues
- [ ] Scaffolding reduces with experience

 {"id":"brenner_bot-rffy","title":"FEATURE: Pre-Registration Lock - Prediction Sealing Before Evidence","description":"
## The Anti-Rationalization Mechanism

This is the SINGLE MOST CRITICAL feature for methodological integrity. Without prediction locking, users can always claim post-hoc that they "knew it all along." This defeats the entire purpose of discriminative testing.

### Core Mechanism
- **Lock Predictions Before Evidence**: User makes specific predictions, then LOCKS them
- **Cryptographic Sealing**: Hash of prediction + timestamp (prevents tampering)
- **Visual Lock Status**: Clear UI showing locked vs. unlocked predictions
- **Unlock Penalty**: Post-lock modifications flagged with "amended after evidence"

### Prediction Lock States
1. **Draft** - Prediction can be freely edited
2. **Locked** - Prediction sealed, waiting for evidence
3. **Revealed** - Evidence collected, prediction vs. outcome compared
4. **Amended** - User modified after evidence (flagged, credibility hit)

### Key Invariants
- Once locked, the original prediction is IMMUTABLE
- Users CAN add notes/amendments, but original is preserved
- Timestamp is part of the seal (proves when prediction was made)
- Robustness score penalizes post-hoc amendments

### Implementation Notes
- Store: original_prediction, lock_timestamp, lock_hash
- UI: Lock button that requires confirmation ("I understand I cannot change this")
- Visual: Locked predictions show 🔒 icon, greyed out text, "Sealed on [date]"
- Comparison view: Show prediction vs. outcome side-by-side when revealed

### Why This Matters
Brenner's entire methodology depends on DISCRIMINATIVE predictions - predictions that would come out DIFFERENTLY under different hypotheses. If users can modify predictions after seeing results, there's no discrimination. This feature is the methodological backbone.
